verses.text: God speaks again: arise, go up to Bethel, dwell there, and make an altar to the God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother. Time to keep the vow made twenty years ago.

Jacob calls his household together: put away the foreign gods among you, purify yourselves, change your clothes. Even Rachel's stolen idols still need clearing out. They give him their gods and earrings, and he buries them under an oak. Some idols need to be put in the ground before worship can rise.

They give him their gods and earrings, and he buries them under an oak. Some idols need to be put in the ground before worship can rise.

Jacob calls his household together: put away the foreign gods among you, purify yourselves, change your clothes. Even Rachel's stolen idols still need clearing out. They give him their gods and earrings, and he buries them under an oak. Some idols need to be put in the ground before worship can rise.

As they journey, a terror of God falls upon the surrounding cities. No one pursues them — even after what happened in Shechem. God shelters them through a region that should have been bristling for revenge.

They reach Luz, also called Bethel. Jacob builds an altar and calls the place El-bethel — the God of Bethel — because here God appeared to him when he was fleeing from his brother. The vow of chapter twenty-eight is finally kept.

They reach Luz, also called Bethel. Jacob builds an altar and calls the place El-bethel — the God of Bethel — because here God appeared to him when he was fleeing from his brother. The vow of chapter twenty-eight is finally kept.

Deborah, Rebekah's old nurse, dies and is buried under an oak below Bethel. Jacob names it Allon-bachuth — oak of weeping. The chapter pauses to mark a small grief. Genesis takes notice of the women others might forget.
God appears to Jacob again and blesses him. Thy name shall no more be called Jacob; thy name shall be Israel. The renaming from Peniel is confirmed and made official. The supplanter is past.
God appears to Jacob again and blesses him. Thy name shall no more be called Jacob; thy name shall be Israel. The renaming from Peniel is confirmed and made official. The supplanter is past.

I am God Almighty — El Shaddai. Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings from your loins. The land given to Abraham and Isaac, I give to you. The covenant is poured over the new name.

I am God Almighty — El Shaddai. Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings from your loins. The land given to Abraham and Isaac, I give to you. The covenant is poured over the new name.
And God went up from him in the place where He talked with him. The encounter ends without ceremony. Some of the most important moments end quietly.
Jacob sets up a stone pillar where God had spoken, pours a drink offering and oil over it. He names the place Bethel — house of God — again. Some places need to be marked more than once.
Jacob sets up a stone pillar where God had spoken, pours a drink offering and oil over it. He names the place Bethel — house of God — again. Some places need to be marked more than once.

They journey from Bethel. Near Ephrath, Rachel goes into hard labor. The midwife tries to comfort her: fear not, this son also. As her soul is departing — she is dying — she names him Ben-oni, son of my sorrow. Jacob renames him Benjamin, son of the right hand. The mother's name speaks her pain; the father's name speaks his hope.

The mother's name speaks her pain; the father's name speaks his hope.

They journey from Bethel. Near Ephrath, Rachel goes into hard labor. The midwife tries to comfort her: fear not, this son also. As her soul is departing — she is dying — she names him Ben-oni, son of my sorrow. Jacob renames him Benjamin, son of the right hand. The mother's name speaks her pain; the father's name speaks his hope.

Rachel dies and is buried on the road to Ephrath — which is Bethlehem. Jacob sets a pillar on her grave that stands unto this day. The favorite wife is the first of the matriarchs to die before old age, and the only one not buried in Machpelah.

Rachel dies and is buried on the road to Ephrath — which is Bethlehem. Jacob sets a pillar on her grave that stands unto this day. The favorite wife is the first of the matriarchs to die before old age, and the only one not buried in Machpelah.
Israel journeys on and pitches his tent beyond the tower of Edar. The text gives him a single line of forward motion after the deepest grief of his life. Faith keeps walking.

And while Israel dwelt in that land, Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine. Israel heard. That is all the text says — and yet Reuben will lose his firstborn rights for this. The wounds compound. Genesis closes the verse abruptly: now the sons of Jacob were twelve.

The twelve are listed by mother. Leah's: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun. Rachel's: Joseph, Benjamin. Bilhah's: Dan, Naphtali. Zilpah's: Gad, Asher. Israel's whole future is in these four columns.

The twelve are listed by mother. Leah's: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun. Rachel's: Joseph, Benjamin. Bilhah's: Dan, Naphtali. Zilpah's: Gad, Asher. Israel's whole future is in these four columns.

Jacob finally reaches his father Isaac at Mamre — the place of Abraham. Isaac is one hundred and eighty years old when he dies, gathered to his people, old and full of days. Esau and Jacob bury him together. The brothers' reconciliation holds long enough for a shared grave.
